coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-based fluorescent layered double hydroxide for targeted drug delivery application
coredata.dc:creator: Molaei, Mohammad Jafar
coredata.dc:description: Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) are a class of layered materials that have received great attention as drug delivery carriers due to their excellent properties such as biocompatibility, chemical stability, easy synthesis, anion exchange capacity, and controlled drug release. In this research, LDH nanosheets based on Ca and Al were synthesized by a coprecipitation method. The LDH nanosheets were in situ coated by polyethylene glycol (PEG). The LDH nanosheets were decorated with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from citric acid by a hydrothermal method and targeted with folic acid. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) was used as a model drug to investigate the drug loading and release of the fluorescent LDH. XRD analysis showed that the content of PEG in the synthesis medium affects the crystallinity of the LDHs. FTIR analysis confirmed the successful attachment of CQDs and folic acid (FA) on LDH nanosheets. The fluorescence of the synthesized nanohybrid was influenced by the CQDs content. UV–Vis spectroscopy showed the DOX can be loaded on the nanohybrid with a loading efficiency of 32 %. The drug release of the nanohybrid is pH-dependent. The drug can be released up to 83 % within 12 h in the acidic environment while the release reaches 63 % in the physiological pH at the same time. The synthesized CQDs can be used as pH-dependent fluorescent and targeted nanocarriers in drug delivery systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Layered double hydroxides
Carbon quantum dots
Polyethylene glycol
Fluorescence
Drug delivery
Folic acid

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author would like to appreciate the Shahrood University of Technology and the Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative Council for their financial support of this project.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the synthesis method for the preparation of the drug-loaded fluorescent LDH nanosheets.
XRD patterns of the (a) LDH-0 sample and the nanocomposite samples of (b) LDH-1, (c) LDH-2, and (d) LDH-4.

(a) AFM image of the synthesized CQDs, (b) their corresponding size distribution histogram and mean particle size, (c) UV–visible absorption, and (d) PL emission spectra of the CQDs by the excitation at wavelengths in the range of 300–400 nm.
The FTIR spectra of pure (a) LDH-0, (b) LDH-4, (c) LDH/CQDs-1, (d) LDH/CQDs-2, (e) LDH/CQDs-4, (f) LDH/CQDs/FA, and (g) LDH/CQDs/FA/DOX samples.
FESEM images of the synthesized (a and b) LDH-0 sample at different magnifications, (c) LDH-1 sample, and (d) LDH-4 sample.
(a) FESEM image of the synthesized LDH, (b) corresponding EDS spectrum, and (c) its elemental distribution map.

PL spectra of the LDH/CQDs nanocomposite samples by excitation at different wavelengths in the range of 300–400 nm, (a) LDH/CQDs-1, (b) LDH/CQDs-2, (c) LDH/CQDs-3, and (d) LDH/CQDs-4 samples, (e) LDH-4 and LDH/CQDs/FA samples, and water under daylight and (f) under UV lamp.
PL spectra of LDH/CQDs nanocomposite samples of LDH/COD-1, LDH/COD-2, LDH/COD-3, and LDH/COD-4 by excitation at (a) 320 nm, (b) 340 nm, (c) 360 nm, and (d) 380 nm.
Cumulative drug release of the LDH/CQDs/FA/DOX nanocomposite at different pHs of 5.5 and 7.4.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-based fluorescent layered double hydroxide for targeted drug delivery application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shahrood University of Technology, Shahrood 3619995161, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shahrood University of Technology, Shahrood 3619995161, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) are a class of layered materials that have received great attention as drug delivery carriers due to their excellent properties such as biocompatibility, chemical stability, easy synthesis, anion exchange capacity, and controlled drug release. In this research, LDH nanosheets based on Ca and Al were synthesized by a coprecipitation method. The LDH nanosheets were in situ coated by polyethylene glycol (PEG). The LDH nanosheets were decorated with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from citric acid by a hydrothermal method and targeted with folic acid. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) was used as a model drug to investigate the drug loading and release of the fluorescent LDH. XRD analysis showed that the content of PEG in the synthesis medium affects the crystallinity of the LDHs. FTIR analysis confirmed the successful attachment of CQDs and folic acid (FA) on LDH nanosheets. The fluorescence of the synthesized nanohybrid was influenced by the CQDs content. UV–Vis spectroscopy showed the DOX can be loaded on the nanohybrid with a loading efficiency of 32 %. The drug release of the nanohybrid is pH-dependent. The drug can be released up to 83 % within 12 h in the acidic environment while the release reaches 63 % in the physiological pH at the same time. The synthesized CQDs can be used as pH-dependent fluorescent and targeted nanocarriers in drug delivery systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a major cause of mortality worldwide. Cancer arises due to abnormal cell division and heterogeneous cell growth. An emerging approach for cancer treatment is targeted drug delivery which has advantages such as low biodegradability of the drug and high efficiency [1]. Various nanoparticle-based drug carriers including magnetic nanoparticles [2], liposomes [3], solid lipids [4], polymers [5], dendrimers [6], micelles [7], metal and non-metal nanoparticles [8], and carbon dots (CDs) [9] have been used for this purpose.

Among several classes of lamellar solids, LDHs are known as anionic clays or hydrotalcite-like systems since they resemble intercalation compounds. These structures have characteristics such as pH-dependent solubility, high chemical stability, and biocompatibility. The tendency of LDHs for drug release in a determined pH makes them superior compared to other drug delivery systems. LDHs with their inorganic layers can protect, release in the target site, release in the desired manner, and avoid degradation of the drug. Depending on the drug release mechanism, several factors such as pH can affect the release behavior [10]. LDHs are a large class of inorganic host materials with a general structural formula in the form of
M
1 − x
2 +
M x
3 +
OH
2
x +
A
n −
x / n
·zH2O in which M2+ and M3+ stands for metal ions and
A
n −
refers to guest anion. LDHs have found wide applications in drug delivery, separation process, and catalysis [11]. In contrast to most cationic clays with negatively charged layers, the charge density and elemental composition of LDHs can vary in a wide range during the synthesis process [12].

LDHs have been used in the production of fluorescent thin films. For fluorescent sensors, if molecular room-temperature phosphorescent materials are to be used, their efficiency depends on their crystalline rigidity in a host that prevents their intramolecular motion. LDHs are promising candidates for the 2D host for such thin films [13–15]. Thin films of luminescent LDHs with polarized photoemission and prolonged luminescence lifetime have been successfully synthesized [16].

CQDs are carbon nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm that could show fluorescence emission. CQDs have superior properties including chemically inert structure, high-temperature stability, biocompatibility, easy synthesis, and easy functionalization [17,18]. The functional groups such as carboxyl, carbonyl, epoxy, and hydroxyl on the CQDs affect their PL and water solubility. CQDs are resistant to blinking and photobleaching [19]. N-doping has a considerable effect on the PL properties of CQDs [20]. It has been reported that N-doped CQDs have higher quantum yield and photocatalytic activity [21]. CQDs have found several biomedical applications such as bioimaging, cancer chemotherapy, and photothermal therapy [22].

Several anticancer drugs with different chemical characteristics such as cisplatin, doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide, methotrexate, and 5-fluorouracil have been successfully incorporated into LDHs. The therapeutic agent that is incorporated in LDH has better cellular internalization, controlled release, greater chemical stability, and therefore, enhanced bioavailability compared to the free agent. Hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, van der Waals forces, and, in some cases, coordinative bonding help the interlayer molecules to be stabilized [23]. Furthermore, the positively charged LDHs can be internalized into the tumor and release the interlayer drug molecules in response to the tumor's acidic microenvironment due to the protonation of the LDHs' OH groups around metal ions [24].

Folic acid has many receptors on the epithelial tumor cells, especially the ovary, colon, breast, brain uterus, and endometrium. These receptors are anchored on the tumor cells and show a high affinity for FA. Furthermore, FA molecule can easily conjugate to the surface of drug carriers, has a small size, and is safe. Therefore, FA has been used widely as a targeting molecule for the active compound delivery to cancer cells through the receptor-ligand complex [25].

In this research, fluorescent LDH based on Al and Ca were synthesized by a precipitation method. To increase the biocompatibility of the LDH nanosheets, PEG was used during in-situ synthesis. The LDH nanosheets were decorated with N-doped CQDs. The N-doped-CQDs were synthesized hydrothermally from citric acid and PEI. Folic acid was used for the targeted delivery of the nanocarrier. The PL performance and drug release of the synthesized LDH were investigated.

Calcium nitrate tetrahydrate (Ca(NO3)2·4H2O), aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (Al(NO3)3·9H2O), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH), polyethylene glycol 600, methanol (CH3OH), folic acid, and citric acid (C6H8O7) were provided from Merck. Polyethyleneimine, linear, MW 25000, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

CQDs were synthesized with a hydrothermal method from citric acid as the carbon source and PEI as the N-doping agent. For the synthesis of CQDs, 2 g citric acid was dissolved in 30 ml of deionized water. Then, 500 μl of PEI was added to the solution followed by stirring for 10 min. The solution of citric acid and PEI was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. The autoclave was cooled down to room temperature and a yellowish solution was achieved. The solution was dialyzed against deionized water with a 2 kDa dialysis tube to remove unreacted species. The dialysis was performed by dialyzing 30 ml of the CQDs solution in a 10 cm length dialysis tube and continued for 3 days. The outside deionized water (a total of 3 l) in the container was refreshed every 6 h for 1 day and every 12 h for the next 2 days. Finally, the solution inside the dialysis tube was collected for further synthesis steps.

For the synthesis of PEG-coated AlCa LDH, 0.45 g of Al(NO3)3·9H2O and 0.85 g of Ca(NO3)2·4H2O were added to a totally 20 ml solution of methanol and deionized water mixture with Vmethanol/Vdeionized water = 1:1. The solution was stirred for 30 min to ensure full dissolution of the metal salts (solution A). For the preparation of the PEG solution, 10 ml of PEG was added to 20 ml of deionized water and stirred until complete dissolution and reaching a transparent solution (Solution B). Then, 0.48 g NaOH and 0.32 g Na2CO3 were added to 100 ml of deionized water and stirred until the complete dissolution of the salts (solution C). For the synthesis of PEG-coated AlCa LDH, different amounts of PEG solution (solution B) were added to solution A. Subsequently, solution C was added dropwise to solution A until the pH reached 10 under vigorous stirring. The suspension continued stirring for 15 min followed by sonication for 10 min and repeated stirring for 2 h. Then the precipitates were washed with deionized water and collected with a 4000-rpm centrifuge machine several times until the pH reached neutral followed by drying at 60 °C overnight. This synthesis procedure was repeated for 1, 2, 4, and 10 ml of PEG-containing solution (solution B) that was added to the reaction container, and the samples were labeled as LDH-1, LDH-2, LDH-3, and LDH-4, respectively. The control sample was synthesized without adding PEG solution in the synthesis procedure and was labeled as LDH-0.

Among the LDH samples, the LDH-4 sample was chosen for the synthesis of LDH/CQDs nanocomposites. For each LDH/CQDs sample, 0.5 g of LDH nanosheets was dispersed in 10 ml of deionized water. Different amounts of CQDs solution were added to the LDH suspension and stirred for 8 h. Samples with different amounts of 1 ml, 2 ml, 4 ml, and 10 ml of CQDs solution were synthesized and were labeled as LDH/CQDs-1, LDH/CQDs-2, LDH/CQDs-3, and LDH/CQDs-4, respectively.

For the conjugation of FA to LDH/CQDs, 100 mM EDC, and 300 mg of FA were dissolved in 25 ml of PBS. Then, 100 mM NHS was added to the solution and stirred in the dark for 2 h. Then 1 g of LDH/CQDs-4 sample was dispersed in the activated FA solution and stirred for 15 h at dark. The FA-conjugated LDH/CQDs nanohybrid was washed with deionized water, collected with a 4000-rpm centrifuge, and dried at 50 °C overnight.

For drug loading, 0.8 g of LDH/CQDs/FA was dispersed in a 25 ml PBS solution. Then 5 ml of DOX drug solution (containing 2 mg/ml doxorubicin hydrochloride) was added to the suspension and stirred in the dark for 24 h. Subsequently, the suspension was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min to leave unbounded moieties in the supernatant. The concentration of the drug in the supernatant was evaluated by UV–Vis spectroscopy. The drug loading efficiency was calculated according to the following equation:
(1)
Loading efficiency
%
=
Total amount of drug − Free drug in the supernatant
/ Total amount of drug
× 100

The drug loading capacity was also measured according to the following formula:
(2)
Drug loading capacity
%
=
Total amount of loaded drug / total amount of carrier
× 100

The drug release of the synthesized nanohybrid was studied in PBS solution at different pHs of 7.4 and 5.5. For each of investigated pH conditions, 0.3 g of drug-loaded nanohybrid was kept in a dialysis tube (MWCO of 12 kDa) containing 10 ml PBS. The tube was immersed in 25 ml PBS and kept for a certain time. Samples of volume 3 ml were collected and replenished with the same volume of fresh PBS at different time intervals. The drug releases were calculated by UV-absorption spectroscopy using the absorbance at 480 nm. The following equation was used to calculate the drug release:
(3)
Drug release
%
=
Total amount of released drug / The amount of loaded drug
× 100
Fig. 1 shows a schematic of the synthesis method for the preparation of the drug-loaded fluorescent LDH nanosheets.

Phase analysis was performed by a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with a step size of 0.02° in the 2θ range of 5–90°. The basal distance between LDH nanosheets was calculated according to the Scherrer formula [26]. The chemical bonding of the samples was investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Rayleigh WQF-520) in the wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm−1. The PL of the LDH/CQDs nanocomposites was recorded by a PL spectrometer (Shimadzu-RF 6000). The samples were excited by wavelengths in the range of 300–400 nm and the emission was recorded in the visible range of 400–700 nm. Particles' morphology was studied using a Zeiss Sigma 300-HV FESEM after sputter-coating the samples with gold. The Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) image was provided by Park Scientific Instruments, CP, Auto probe AFM. A drop of CQDs' solution was brought on the recently cleaved mica and let to evaporate at room temperature to leave the dots on the surface. The cleavage was done by the Scotch tape method. A Perkin Elmer Lambda 25 UV–Vis spectrometer was used to record the UV–vis absorption of the samples. The photograph of the fluorescent LDH sample was taken under a UV lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm.

XRD patterns of the LDH-0 sample and the nanocomposite samples of LDH-1, LDH-2, and LDH-4 are shown in Fig. 2 . The XRD pattern of the synthesized LDH sample in Fig. 2a resembles PDF#54-0849 with the chemical formula of Ca4Al2O6((NO3)2·10H2O which is alike to the XRD patterns that have been reported for LDH-like substances in literature [27]. The XRD peaks of the synthesized LDHs are sharp and symmetric at lower angles, but weaker at higher angles which is typical for layered LDH structures [28]. Due to the preferred orientation of the crystallites in LDHs, only 001 reflections can be seen in the XRD patterns [29]. The basal spacing, d003 for the synthesized LDHs is 8.5 Å which is in good agreement with those that have been reported before [30]. It can be seen that by increasing the PEG content of the reaction chamber, the peak intensity decreases, and the peak width increases. These changes in the XRD peaks could be due to the decrease in the crystallinity which might be evidence of intercalation of the LDH nanosheets. For the LDH-4 sample, the basal plane peak of 003 has been disappeared. This might be due to the exfoliation of the nanosheets.

Fig. 3 shows the AFM image, size distribution histogram, mean particle size, UV–vis absorption, and PL spectra of the synthesized CQDs. The AFM image of CQDs and their corresponding size distribution histogram illustrates that the synthesized dots have almost sizes between 4 and 7 nm with a mean particle size of 6 nm. The synthesized CQDs show maximum UV absorption at 360 nm. This broad peak is related to n → π* electronic transitions [31,32]. The PL spectra of the CQDs reveal that they have emission in the visible range by excitation with wavelengths in the range of 300–400 nm. The highest PL intensity has been obtained by excitation at 380 nm. The spectra confirm that the PL is excitation-wavelength dependent which is characteristic of CQDs [17].

Fig. 4 shows the FTIR spectra of pure LDH-0, LDH-4, LDH/CQDs-1, LDH/CQDs-2, LDH/CQDs-4, LDH/CQDs/FA, and LDH/CQDs/FA/DOX samples. The strong band in the FTIR spectrum of the LDH-0 sample centered at 3490 cm−1 is attributed to adsorbed water molecules and hydroxyl groups. The characteristic adsorption band of NO3 − intercalated LDH can be detected at 1376 cm−1 for the ν3 modes of interlayer NO3 −. The peaks at 424 cm−1, 524 cm−1, and 786 cm−1 are related to MO lattice vibrations. The band in the low-frequency region of 400–700 cm−1 which is due to the metal‑oxygen bond are typical peaks that are observed in this kind of layered solid. The presence of H2O molecules as bending vibrations can be concluded from the peak at 1623 cm−1 which is due to the bending mode of interlayer water molecules i.e. δ (H-OH) [33]. The peak at 1353 cm−1 is attributed to the existence of trace CO3 2− [34]. The peak at 2919 cm−1 for the PEG-coated LDH is attributed to the alkyl chain of the polymer [35]. The LDH absorption bands can also be seen in the PEG-coated LDH. However, the more intense peak at 3633 cm−1 could be attributed to the presence of OH bonds of PEG molecules or more adsorbed water molecules due to the PEG coating. For the samples that are decorated with CQDs, the absorption bands of LDH and PEG are observed. Furthermore, the peaks in the range of 2860–2950 cm−1 are attributed to the CH bond [36]. The peak at 1078 cm−1 is also related to COC vibrations [37]. The peaks at 1070–1105 cm−1 are due to CO stretching. The peak at 1644 cm−1 is attributed to (OCNH/CC) stretching vibrations [38].

The FTIR spectrum of LDHs/CQDs/FA also shows bands between 3600 and 3000 cm−1 that are attributed to the NH– stretching and –OH stretching vibrations [39]. The peak at 1620 cm−1 is also attributed to the aromatic ring stretch of the pteridine ring and p-amino benzoic acid moieties of FA [40]. After conjugation of DOX to LDH/CQDs/FA, the related absorption band of the drug can be seen in the FTIR spectrum at 1550 and 1797 cm−1.

Fig. 5 shows the FESEM images of the LDH, LDH-1, and LDH-4 samples. The image of the synthesized LDH sample in Fig. 5a confirms that it consists of plates with lateral sizes in the range of microns and thicknesses below 100 nm. The morphology and polygon shape of the plates of this sample (Fig. 5b) suggest that there is a crystallinity within the layers. For the sample of LDH-1 (Fig. 5c), similar morphology can be seen. However, for the sample of LDH-4 (Fig. 5d), the sizes and thicknesses of the nanosheets have decreased. Furthermore, the LDH-4 sample is better delaminated which is in agreement with their smaller thicknesses. It seems that the higher content of PEG in the synthesis container of the LDH-4 sample has resulted in the intercalation and thinner plates.

Fig. 6 is the FESEM image as well as the EDS spectrum and elemental map of the synthesized LDH. The EDS confirms the presence of Ca, Al, and O in the sample which are the main constituents of the LDH nanosheets. Furthermore, the uniform distribution of these elements can be concluded from EDS results.

The PL spectra of the LDH/CODs nanocomposite samples by excitation at different wavelengths in the range of 300–400 nm are represented in Fig. 7(a–d). The LDH/CQDs/FA sample in comparison to LDH-4 nanosheet suspension and water under daylight and UV irradiation are also exhibited in Fig. 7(e and f). The nanocomposite samples show different PL peak maximums by excitation at different wavelengths. Excitation wavelength-dependent PL is characteristic of CQDs which is observed in their synthesized LDH/CQDs nanocomposites as well. The PL intensity for all nanocomposite samples is lower for excitation wavelengths of 300–340 and 400 nm. The PL intensity is highest for the excitation wavelength of 360 nm for all nanocomposite samples. The comparison of PL intensity for different samples in Fig. 7 shows that for the samples with different CQDs content, the PL intensity is dependent on the CQDs content during the synthesis of the nanocomposites. The PL intensity is highest for LDH/COD-4 sample as can be concluded from PL spectra with excitation wavelengths of 320, 340, 360, and 380 nm in Fig. 8 . Excitation wavelength-dependent PL of the CQDs is due to the presence of CQDs with different sizes, different surface chemistry, and emissive traps in a synthesis batch. Fluorescence in CQDs can occur due to the radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs in the sp2 clusters. Different sizes of the sp2 clusters result in different bandgaps of these clusters. Therefore, the PL spectrum of the CQDs can range from visible to near-infrared regions of the spectrum. Furthermore, the functional groups on the CQDs surface which are extremely dependent on the synthesis method also result in the formation of emissive traps on the CQDs and affect their PL characteristics [41].

The drug loading and release of the synthesized LDH/CQDs/FA/DOX nanohybrid were evaluated by using DOX as the model drug. The DOX could be loaded up to 32 % on the synthesized nanohybrid. The drug loading capacity was calculated to be 17 %. The release behavior of the synthesized nanohybrid at normal and acidic pH conditions are represented in Fig. 9 . In normal pH condition, DOX can be released up to 63 % within 12 h. However, in acidic pH conditions, DOX was released 83 % within the same period. The pH-responsive of functionalized LDH hybrids in the body environment has been reported before [42]. Magnesium–aluminum-layered double hydroxide/PEG-coated superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles could release anticancer drug sorafenib 84 % and 40 % in 4500 min at pH of 4.8 and 7.4, respectively. The drug loading capacity of ZIF-67@Co-LDH yolk-shell spheres with micro-/meso-porous structures has been reported to be 1.26 % [43]. The release of ibuprofen from Er3+-doped ZnAl-LDH has shown to be 68.37 % within 6 h [44]. The drug loading capacity in our study is comparable to other similar works and the pH-responsive release is prolonged.

Many trivalent and divalent metal cations that are used in the formation of LDHs have low toxicity. For example, MgAl-LDH-CO3 is used as an antacid which is evidence of its low toxicity. In addition, it has been shown that the intercalation of pharmaceutical drugs into the layers, improves the internalization of the drug into a target cell without any noticeable side effects. LDH nanoparticles are not accumulated in the organs after administration and are rapidly excreted from the body, showing their efficacy as biodegradable drug delivery nanocarriers [45]. Therefore, multifunctional drug carrier such as LDH/CQDs/FA/DOX nanohybrid in this research is promising for simultaneous chemotherapy and bioimaging.

In this research, DOX-loaded fluorescent LDH/CQDs nanohybrid was synthesized and FA was used for targeting the nanocarrier. PEG was used in the synthesis of the nanohybrid for coating and intercalation into LDH layers. The synthesized nanohybrid showed fluorescence by excitation at different wavelengths in the range of 300–400 nm. The LDH/CQDs/FA nanohybrid showed pH-dependent drug release which was higher in an acidic environment. This is an advantage for faster release of the drug in the acidic microenvironment of the tumor site. The synthesized LDH/CQDs/FA/DOX nanohybrid is promising for multifunctional chemotherapy and bioimaging due to fluorescence emission.

Mohammad Jafar Molaei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author would like to appreciate the Shahrood University of Technology and the Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative Council for their financial support of this project.
